text.title: Recognition Over Recall Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every recognition-over-recall application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Prefer recognition-based UI patterns over recall-based ones whenever design permits. Recall is only acceptable for power-user surfaces with confirmed user expectation.
rule.rationale: Recognition has lower cognitive cost and faster reaction time; defaulting to recall externalises load onto users.

rule.statement: Make options visible: show common actions in toolbars / navigation. Do NOT hide them in right-click menus or kebab overflows.
rule.rationale: Hidden actions are undiscoverable for users who do not know to look for them.

rule.statement: In multi-step flows, show a persistent summary of all prior selections visible alongside the current step.
rule.rationale: Cross-screen recall burden is invisible in static reviews; it shows up in usability testing as repeated back-button use.

rule.statement: Pair every icon with a text label. Tooltips alone are insufficient; use tooltips only in space-constrained areas where label cannot fit.
rule.rationale: Icons-only require recall of meaning; even "universal" icons fail across populations.

rule.statement: Tooltips MUST be ≤7 words, shown on hover with a 300ms delay. Longer tooltips are documentation, not affordance.
rule.rationale: Longer tooltips become unreadable in flow; tooltips compete with help.

rule.statement: Add autocomplete to every search and command input; show at least 5 recent items immediately on focus.
rule.rationale: Recents and suggestions convert recall to recognition, the highest-ROI UX change for search surfaces.

rule.statement: Recent items MUST update immediately after use. A stale list is worse than no list because it teaches users not to trust it.
rule.rationale: Trust in the recents UI is fragile; one stale entry loses the affordance.

rule.statement: The audit MUST consume an icon allowlist (universally recognised exceptions: magnifying-glass, X-close, etc.). Findings against allowlisted icons are dropped to severity-0 (informational) only.
rule.rationale: Without an allowlist the audit drowns in noise for icons that are in fact recognised.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
